The Aramaic word abba, sometimes 2ppa (father, hence abbot), Evagopmtic anachorétds anchorite has been retained in its original form, as it was in the Greek text (cf Eoxnorc askésis asceticism Mark 1436, Romans 815; Gal. 4:6). Abba usually means a monk, Sraxpmoc diakrisis discernment but it is sometimes used (in the form abbas) to mean the chief monk Eyxierato egilistos recluse (c. 54, c. 80), in which case it could be translated w abbot. Abba Eonpoc erémos wilderness® can oven designate a bishop, presumably a monk-bishop (c. 79), but Hovxzothc hésuchastés solitary there is some indication that towards the ead of the seventh century Beiog theios godly it was beginning to be used geacrally of clerics, much like the b tv ayiore ho en hagiois saintly French abbd. ) ) otxovopla oikonomia providence

It appears that both John and his disciple Sophronios, the JOHN MOSCHOS future Patriarch of Jerusalem, hailed from Damascus. Sophronios John Moschos, surnamed Eucrates, is obviously more than a name, was almost certainly born there’, and at one point (c. 171) John although the task of discovering what lies behind the name is not an speaks of their affection for a certain Zoilos: ‘because we shared the easy one. There are two documents which supply the main outlines same homeland and upbringing’ <81& x3 xowdy Zrew of John’s life, including a few valuable details: a brief anonymous ratpida xal viv &vaorpopfiv.> Unfortunately, if this biography’ and the description of Pratum Spiritusle in Photios’ indicates where John was born, it gives no indication of whea. It Bibliotheca (Cod.199) composed in the second half of the ninth can be assumed that he was somewhat older than Sophronios since century. But by far the greater part of our knowledge of John is to they seem to have stood in the relationship of spiritual father and be gleaned from his own work, of which the Prafum is almost the son. One does not have lo be an exact contemporary to share only surviving example. But as John was somebody who ‘went to the same school’, ot came from the same not in the least concerned to write about himself, these may easily town-——albeit a generation later or carlier than oneself. xx The Spiritual Meadow It should however be noted that this date is by no means certain. We have a firm statement that Sophronios came to Jerusalem with the remains of his friend ‘at the beginning of the eighth indiction”. That could mean either September 619 or September 634. For the most part, learned opinion has tended to accept the earlier date, but a recent study of the matter has shown JOHN MOSCHOS EUCRATES that it is still not possible to state with certainty which is the correct + one. THE SPIRITUAL MEADOW A NOTE ON THE SIGNS USED IN THIS TEXT (Pratum spirituale) [...] indicates text which is found in the Latin text but not in the Greek, but please note that since all the titles of the chapters fall into this category, these have not been placed in square brackets. <..> indicate words added by the translator which are either necessary to make the meaning clear or desirable to fill a real or suspected lacuna in the text.
An asterisk (*) indicates that there is a brief note on the word or passage by which an asterisk stands. Please see pages 231-256 for the endnotes.

There is a general note on money and currency beginning on page 231. © Eorica Follieri, ‘Dove ¢ quando mort Giovanni Moseo? Rivists di Studi Birzaotial ¢ Neoelleaici NS 25 (1988) 3.39, seeks to demonstrate that John Moschos died at Roms after 1) November 620 but before spring-summer 634,
JOBN EVIRATUS TO HIS BELOVED IN CHRIST, SOPHRONIOS THE SOPHIST

In my opinion, the meadows in spring preseat a particularly delightful prospect. They display to the beholder a rich diversity of flowers which arrests him with its charm, for it brings delight to his eyes and perfume to his nostrils. One part of this meadow blushes with roses; in another place Jilies predominate, drawing one's attention to themselves and away from the roses. In another part the colour of violets blazes out, resembling the imperial purple. In short, the diversity and variety of innumerable flowers affords delights both to nostril and to eye on every side.

Think of this present werk in the same way Sophronios, my sacred and faithful child. For in it, you will discover the virtues of holy men who have distinguished themselves in our own times; men, as the Psalmist says, planted by the waterside <Ps 1:3>. They were all equally beloved of God (by the grace of Christ),—yet there was a diversity in the virtues from which the beauty and the charm of each derived. From among these I have plucked the finest flowers of the unmown meadow and worked them into a crown which I now offer to you, most [laithful child; and through you, to the world at large.

1 have called this work meadow on account of the delight, the fragrance and the benefit which it will afford those who come across it. For the virtuous life and habitual piety do not merely consist of studying divinity; not only of thinking on an elevated plain about things as they are here and now. It must also include the description in writing of the way of lif of others. So I have striven to complete

As he was unable to walk, they found a small cave and went into At the monastery of our holy father Sabas* we met Athanasios. The it so that the elder could rest. Saint John then made him sit down on one of the ‘Now you are doing well, brother, and looking after your own soul’. hills, stripped him of his clothes and three times made the sign of He said to me: ‘Abba, I am about to die sir’,—and three days later the cross beneath his navel. ‘Believe me, Conon the priest’, he said, he was dead.

‘I wanted you to carry away some reward from the struggle. But This same Polychronios, priest of the New Lavra, also told me since you did not wish it to be so, I have caused the struggle to this: Once whilst I was staying at the Lavra of the Towers one of cease. But you shall have no reward for this’. Conon the priest the brothers died. The steward said to me: ‘Of your charity, brother, returned to the task of baptising at the community and next day he come so we can carry that brother’s effects into the storeroom’. As baptised and anointed the Persian withoul even being aware that she we began to move his things I saw the steward weeping. I said to was of the female sex. For twelve years he anointed and baptised him: "Come now, abba, why are you weeping in this way, sir’? He without suffering any physica) disturbance and with no awareness replied: ‘Because today I am carrying out that brother's effects and of women’s femininity; so he drew his life to a close. two days from now others shall bear away mine’. And so it was; two days later the steward himself died, just as he said.
